  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Which focus should I use to “make this stronger”? Choose one: (A) Style polish only—tighten wording and flow while keeping claims, numbers, and structure unchanged; (B) Argument/contribution strengthening—reframe the thesis and novelty, adjust claim scope (e.g., “first,” “any,” “no advantage”) for supportability, and ensure logic/evidence alignment with minimal reordering; (C) Full section-level rework—reorganize the narrative and foreground the contribution, potentially moving sentences/claims across paragraphs in addition to polish.",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Strengthening likely requires reframing the thesis and contribution positioning around the ‘reliability frontier’ to sharpen the central claim.",
    "R1.2": "The passage makes strong inferences (e.g., market eligibility and lack of advantage) that should be checked for internal logical coherence.",
    "R1.3": "Specific threshold and feasibility claims must align tightly with the reported curves/tables to support stronger assertions.",
    "R1.4": "Claims of being the ‘first analytical tool’ and broad applicability require careful novelty scoping versus nearest prior work.",
    "R1.6": "Clearer, more concise sentences and stronger phrasing will directly increase persuasive force without changing content.",
    "RX.1": "Strengthening should transparently flag uncertainties or limits (e.g., seeds, markets, protocols) to avoid overstating generality.",
    "RX.2": "Avoid overreach in universal or superlative claims (e.g., ‘first’, ‘any’, ‘no practical advantage’) unless fully supported.",
    "RX.3": "Work should stay within the scope of revising this passage rather than introducing new experiments or unprovided evidence.",
    "RX.4": "Cautious framing reduces risk of misleading practitioners or policymakers about market eligibility and reliability.",
    "RX.5": "Any strengthened text should keep traceable links to the original statements, figures, and defined terms in the artifact."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: Which focus should I use to “make this stronger”? Choose one: (A) Style polish only—tighten wording and flow while keeping claims, numbers, and structure unchanged; (B) Argument/contribution strengthening—reframe the thesis and novelty, adjust claim scope (e.g., “first,” “any,” “no advantage”) for supportability, and ensure logic/evidence alignment with minimal reordering; (C) Full section-level rework—reorganize the narrative and foreground the contribution, potentially moving sentences/claims across paragraphs in addition to polish.

rationale.R1.1: Strengthening likely requires reframing the thesis and contribution positioning around the ‘reliability frontier’ to sharpen the central claim.
rationale.R1.2: The passage makes strong inferences (e.g., market eligibility and lack of advantage) that should be checked for internal logical coherence.
rationale.R1.3: Specific threshold and feasibility claims must align tightly with the reported curves/tables to support stronger assertions.
rationale.R1.4: Claims of being the ‘first analytical tool’ and broad applicability require careful novelty scoping versus nearest prior work.
rationale.R1.6: Clearer, more concise sentences and stronger phrasing will directly increase persuasive force without changing content.
rationale.RX.1: Strengthening should transparently flag uncertainties or limits (e.g., seeds, markets, protocols) to avoid overstating generality.
rationale.RX.2: Avoid overreach in universal or superlative claims (e.g., ‘first’, ‘any’, ‘no practical advantage’) unless fully supported.
rationale.RX.3: Work should stay within the scope of revising this passage rather than introducing new experiments or unprovided evidence.
rationale.RX.4: Cautious framing reduces risk of misleading practitioners or policymakers about market eligibility and reliability.
rationale.RX.5: Any strengthened text should keep traceable links to the original statements, figures, and defined terms in the artifact.
